title: No IMS bearing (DFI 9A1/MA1 reassurance)
symptoms: None — this is a reassurance note, not a fault
description: The 981 uses the DFI 9A1 / MA1 flat-six, which has NO intermediate shaft (IMS) and therefore no IMS bearing. The camshafts are chain-driven directly off the crankshaft with the timing chains at the front of the engine. The IMS bearing failures that plagued the older M96/M97 engines in the 986/987.1 simply do not exist on the 981.
fix: Nothing to do — there is no IMS bearing to replace or worry about.

title: Bore / cylinder scoring (DFI flat-six)
affected: All 9A1 variants; front cylinders (1 & 4) most cited. Far rarer on the 981 than on the older 996/997.1/987.1.
description: Cylinder bore scoring is a known watercooled-flat-six concern but is rare on the 981. It is hard to hear over the normal DFI injector tick, so it is best confirmed with a borescope or used-oil analysis rather than by ear. Theorized contributors include tight cold clearances and oil aeration.
fix: No fix short of an engine rebuild or long-block replacement.

title: Water pump failure
affected: All 981; risk rises with age/mileage (watch past ~70k mi)
description: The bearing supporting the pump shaft wears, the shaft wobbles, and it develops into a knocking noise and/or coolant leak. This is a wear item; some pumps use a plastic impeller (a common Porsche concern).
fix: Replace the water pump, often together with the thermostat.

title: Coolant expansion tank cracking
affected: All 981 (the plastic tank embrittles with age and heat)
description: The plastic coolant reservoir develops small cracks that progress to full failure and can dump coolant suddenly, leading to overheating if not caught.

title: Front coolant pipe / line leak
symptoms: Minor coolant leak from the front engine area
description: A plastic coolant pipe connects the front of the engine; the rubber seals between the plastic part and the block degrade over time. Porsche revised the part multiple times.
fix: Replace the pipe and seals with the updated part.

title: Air-oil separator (AOS) failure
affected: All 981 (the 2014+ internal AOS design is also implicated in oil-aeration discussions)
symptoms: White smoke from the exhaust (especially at startup)
Excessive suction at the oil filler cap when removed at idle
description: The AOS membrane/diaphragm fails, pulling oil into the intake to be burned. It can also stress the rear main seal through crankcase over-pressure.
fix: Replace the AOS (an upgraded Motorsport unit is available for track use).

title: Rear main seal (RMS) leak
affected: All 981; manual cars worse (leak can foul the clutch)
symptoms: Oil leak at the engine/transmission bellhousing joint
description: The seal at the bellhousing weeps; on manual cars it can contaminate and destroy the clutch. It can be aggravated by AOS-induced crankcase over-pressure.
fix: Drop the transmission and replace the seal (and the clutch on manuals).

title: Ignition coil failure
description: Original ignition coils are prone to cracking. They were superseded by a more robust version.
fix: Replace all six coils with the updated version, ideally with spark plugs.

title: Spark plug seizing on aged / low-mileage cars
affected: All 981, especially low-mileage cars 10+ years old
symptoms: Plugs seized / very hard to remove if left too long
description: Original plugs can seize in the aluminium head if never changed, risking thread damage on removal.
fix: Preventive replacement; do not exceed the interval even on low-mileage cars.

title: High oil consumption
symptoms: Elevated oil consumption (severe cases ~1 qt / 1,000 mi)
description: Some consumption is normal for these flat-sixes; severe cases can tie back to the AOS/PCV system or, rarely, bore wear. Severe warranty cases resulted in engine replacement.
fix: Diagnose the AOS/PCV first, run an oil analysis, and pursue engine work only if severe.

title: PDK Mechatronic unit / sensor issues
description: The PDK gearset itself rarely fails; the electronic/hydraulic Mechatronic clutch unit at the front is the concern. Internal shift-rod position sensors are the most common failure. Regular fluid service is key preventive maintenance.
fix: Repair or replace the Mechatronic, its sensors, or the clutch pack; rarely the full transmission.
estCost: Sensor/repair moderate; Mechatronic replacement several thousand USD

title: Convertible top microswitch / control faults
description: Buried microswitches send top-position signals; when they fail the top will not cycle. The entire top must be removed to reach them. Test the rear-compartment top control unit FIRST — that is a ~5-minute swap versus 6+ hours for switches.
fix: Replace the microswitches (P/N 981-561-943-01) or the control unit.

title: Front control-arm / thrust-arm bushing wear ('coffin arm')
description: The front lower control-arm and thrust-arm rubber bushings wear out. Aftermarket adjustable/monoball kits (e.g. Tarett, BBI) are available as alternatives to the OEM arms.
fix: Replace the arms, or just the bushings.
estCost: ~$400-$1,200 depending on arms vs bushings and labor

title: Strut/shock mount knock (incl. PASM)
affected: All 981; PASM-equipped cars add electronic complexity
symptoms: PASM cars: chassis warning, uneven ride height
description: Upper shock/strut mount rubber dries out and fails. PASM faults can stem from ride-height sensors, leaking struts, or the control system and require electronic diagnosis.
fix: Replace the mounts/struts; diagnose PASM faults electronically.

title: PCM / infotainment board failure
description: Internal PCM (Porsche Communication Management) boards can fail. Board-level repair is often far cheaper than a dealer replacement.
fix: Board-level repair, or replace the unit (dealer).
estCost: ~$1,300-$3,200 (board repair to dealer replacement; low confidence)

title: VarioCam solenoid & vacuum change-over valve failure
symptoms: HVAC flap / heater shutoff / exhaust-flap issues (change-over valve)
description: The variable-valve cam-adjust solenoids can fail (check for wiring corrosion); the vacuum change-over valve (COV) solenoid mis-routes engine vacuum to mechanical systems.

title: Sticking exhaust flap actuator
description: The exhaust-flap actuator rods seize. Porsche often replaces the whole rear silencer; many owners grease the rods preventively.
fix: Lubricate the rods, or replace the silencer/actuator.

title: RECALL: Rear-axle carrier side section may fracture
symptoms: None expected before failure; out-of-spec cast part
description: An out-of-spec rear-axle carrier side section (supplier Brabant Alucast) may fracture. This is a safety recall; dealers replace the part free of charge. Always verify applicability by VIN.
fix: Dealer replaces the affected part free under the recall.

title: RECALL: Airbag control module
affected: 981 covered by a Porsche-initiated airbag-control-module campaign
symptoms: Possible airbag warning; module may not deploy correctly
description: A Porsche-initiated campaign covers assessment/replacement of a defective airbag control module on affected cars. Verify by VIN. Note: a 981-specific Takata airbag campaign was not confirmed in research — always check by VIN.
fix: Dealer assesses and replaces the airbag control module free of charge.
